questionText: Show that the line $y = x + 5$ does not intersect the curve $y = x^2 + 3x + 7$.

steps.description: Set the line equal to the curve.
steps.explanation: Any intersection point must satisfy both equations, so we set the two expressions for $y$ equal.
Bringing all terms to one side gives a quadratic whose real roots would be the $x$-coordinates of any intersections.
Reading off the coefficients prepares us to compute the discriminant.
The sign of the discriminant tells us whether the quadratic has real roots — i.e. whether the graphs meet.
The discriminant is negative, so the quadratic has no real roots.
With no real solutions for $x$, there is no point that lies on both the line and the curve.

steps.workingLatex: \text{the line and curve do not meet}
steps.explanation: Since there are no common points, the line does not intersect the curve.
Rewriting shows the quadratic equals $(x+1)^2 + 1$, which is always at least $1$.
Because the expression is never zero, the equation $x^2 + 2x + 2 = 0$ has no real solutions, agreeing with the discriminant.

steps.workingLatex: \text{the curve lies entirely above the line}
steps.explanation: Since $x^2 + 3x + 7 - (x + 5)$ is always positive, the curve is always above the line, never touching it.

steps.workingLatex: \text{the line } y = x + 5 \text{ does not intersect the curve} \ \blacksquare
steps.explanation: The negative discriminant proves there is no intersection point, as required. $\blacksquare$

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Setting equal gives $x^2 + 2x + 2 = 0$ with $\Delta = 4 - 8 = -4 < 0$, so there are no real solutions and the line does not meet the curve. $\blacksquare$
